The alarm was triggered by vibration and acoustic cavitation detected in pump-1. This was corroborated by a simultaneous pressure sag in the main line and a current spike in pump-1. A pressure transient was detected at timestamp 17, followed by an unexplained increase in flow in the north-zone at timestamp 20. Telemetry at timestamp 24 showed rising pump current alongside falling main pressure. These symptoms, combined with operator notes and the leak runbook, strongly indicate a leak in the main-line. The safe mitigation 'isolate-main-line-segment' was selected to close valves around the suspected leak.

These symptoms, as documented in the leak runbook, strongly indicate a leak in the main-line. The safe mitigation action to isolate the main-line segment was selected.

The alarm was triggered by vibration and acoustic cavitation detected in pump-1. Telemetry showed a simultaneous pressure sag and a current spike in pump-1, which is a classic signature of cavitation as described in the pump runbook. The reservoir level remained stable, ruling out a large-scale leak. The safe mitigation action 'switch-to-pump-2' was selected to shift the load and prevent further damage to pump-1.

The alarm was triggered by vibration and acoustic cavitation detected in pump-1. Telemetry showed a correlated pressure sag and a current spike in pump-1, which is a classic signature of cavitation as described in the pump runbook. The reservoir level remained stable, ruling out a large-scale leak. The recommended mitigation is to switch to pump-2 to alleviate the load on the failing pump.

The incident began with a vibration and acoustic cavitation alarm on pump-1. This was accompanied by a significant current spike in pump-1 and a simultaneous drop in main line pressure. Operator notes confirmed a correlation between the telemetry drift and pump-1. Following the pump runbook, which identifies vibration, current spikes, and reduced pressure as indicators of cavitation, the recommended safe mitigation is to switch the load to pump-2.
